content:
Hi @Danilo Krummrich,

Following up on Thursday’s discussion about Gary’s Io trait changes that are heading upstream soon and may impact the system memory backend: the current implementation is fairly straightforward (see https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/panfrost/linux/-/merge_requests/67) and would help removing some duplication in Tyr's FwInterface implementation. Do you think it'd be possible to merge these changes upstream in the meantime? I’d incorporate @Alice Ryhl ’s pending suggestions before submitting upstream. I haven’t seen Gary’s series yet, but if I understood correctly the changes would simplify the Io trait, so I assume updating the SysMem backend afterwards should be manageable.

Let us know your thoughts, we’d love to keep things moving.

Thanks!
I think it's fine to go ahead and submit on list.
cc @Gary Guo
Does the SysMem has something corresponding to dma_handle in Coherent?
It's just a pointer to memory, and you use volatile ops to read/write
How is it useful if it is not shared with devices?
The device does access the same memory.
Then it is just Coherent?
The latest rework (v3) of my I/O series would allow you to construct a view of Coherent without going through doing an allocation
You just need to provide a CPU pointer + a DMA pointer
What do you mean by a dma pointer?
A dma_addr_t that matches same memory loc as the cpu pointer
I don't see why we need that to write to it from the cpu.
You don't write to it from the cpu, but it is projected together with the CPU pointer.
Anyway, the v3 of I/O series is going to be very different from v2. It's going to be super easy to implement SysMem afterwards.
But we're not going to be using it, though? Letting it be accessed by the gpu is the job of the gem object, not the temporary vmap created into it.
Anyways, when is this series ready? This is blocking the ability to boot the firmware on Tyr, so I'd like to avoid waiting for a rework of the IO traits unless it's going to be very quick.
It really depends on how you use it. If you do a Coherent allocation and hand out subregions to GPU then you need to be able to project through and then get a DMA handle.
The current plan I think is that this is going to land early in the next cycle via a shared topic tag between drm-rust and driver-core @Danilo Krummrich
I'll send out the new version this week
Usually gem objects are mapped into the GPUs address space via GPUVM.
But that's a DRM detail, and doesn't apply for other DMA drivers?
I suppose you could define a method that erases the DMA pointer, and then the erased view type is essentially just sysmem

In general, gem objects are not even guaranteed to have a unique contiguous dma address to the gpu. You could choose to map one range to one place, then another range to another place. The same pages of the gem object could even be mapped into multiple places in the gpu address space. Or if the same gem object is used by multiple userspace processes, then it could appear in several different address spaces from the gpu's perspective.
So talking about a dma address appears to be nonsense to me.

More generally, the same situation appears to apply to dma-buf. The kernel can choose to make a vmap of it if cpu access is required. A single dma-buf can be attached to five different devices, with five different dma addresses.

I think you can remove the GPU virtual address space part from the equation. The relevant part is that there's a different layer taking care of sharing the system memory with the device (e.g. dma-buf), which may use (temporary) streaming DMA mappings. Synchronization is taken care of on the dma-buf / dma-resv / dma-fence layer. So, what we are left with is that we need some representation of this memory, so it can be safely accessed.
Right, so it's really just boiling down to coherent/streaming DMA

Yes, except that the DMA (synchronization) bits are handled on a different layer, which makes this I/O backend a bit more generic. You could theoretically also use it for cases where system memory might be accessed concurrently by multiple CPUs, without a device being involved.

So if I understand correctly there will be a more generic I/O backend exposing a CPU pointer and a DMA pointer (with DMA synchronization handled on a separate layer) and SysMem would then leave the DMA fields unpopulated? (I think I need to look into Coherent<T> and the previous v2 to get proper context first, as I don't have the full picture yet)

SysMem should just be a separate type that does not have DMA pointer. For patch logistics it might be easier that I just include a patch in my series that implement SysMem. Are you okay if I just include SysMem in the next version of I/O projection series and have you as Co-developed-by?
Sounds good to me, thanks Gary! I can then adapt FwInterface in the Tyr driver accordingly. Thanks all for pitching in!
Laura Nao has marked this topic as resolved.
